STATE OF CALIFORNIA-IHE RESOURCES AGENCY
PEIE WILSON, Governor
CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
45 FREMONT, SUITE 2OOO
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105-2219
vorcE AND TDD (4rs) 9O4-'2OO

l
Jar 2l g ol ili '32
January 14, 1992
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Corrnission
P.0. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
RE: Request for Advice Concerning Fees for the San 0nofre Nuclear
Generati ng Stati on Resource l,li ti gat'i on Proj ect
Dear Mr. Ha'llabrin:
Your office has recently prov'ided written advice to a number
officials to address the circumstances under which fees paid
of
to
public
a publ ic
agency and used to retain addit'ional employees or consultants may not be
cons'idered a t'source of income" for those persons so retained. (Savage Adv'ice
Letter No. 90-662a, Alperin Advice Letter No. I-91-324, Egan Advice Letter No.
I-9.l-1.l.l,) In these adv'ice letters, your of f ice concluded that the fees will
not be considered a source of income to employees or consultants reta'ined by
the agency'if certain criteria are satisfied. These criteria are set forth in
the Alperin letter and section 18704.6 of the FPPC's regulations. Subsequent-
ly, an urgency measure became law which amended the Political Reform Act to
add new Government Code section 87.l03.6, which provides, in relevant part,
that a person who makes a payment to a governmental agency to defray the costs
of processing an action shall not be a "source of incomet'to a person retained
by the agency (Chapter 887, Statutes l99l).
lale request adv'ice with respect to the application of these criteria to the
hirinj of scientific consujtants by the Coastal Conrnission as part of the
'independent mon'itoring and adaptive management of the San 0nof re Nuclear
Generating Station (S0NGS) marine resource mitigatjon projects required by the
Coastal Commission as a condition of approval of permit No.183-73. The
mitigation projects are designed to address the damage occurring to marine
resources from the operation of SONGS. The mitigation projects, as well as
the independent monitoring and adaptive management program, are to be funded
entirely by the permittee, Southern Californ'ia Edison, as a condition of the
permi t.
Background
In 1974, the Cal'ifornia Coastal Zone Conservation Commissjon (now, th_t
California Coastal Conmission) authorized construction of Southern California
Edison,s and San D'iego Gas and Electric's Units 2 and 3 of the San Onofre
Nuclear Generating Station (SONGS) in northern San 0iego County. At the time
of the approval, ihe Corrnission lacked sufficient information on the nature


llr. Scott Hal labrin
Janua ry l4, 1992
Page 2
and extent of the impact of the power
environment. To address this lack of
established a Marine Review Collmittee
and 3 on the marine environment.
plant operations on the marine
infomlation, the pennit (No. 183-73)
(liRC) to study the effects of Units 2
Folloriing submission of the flRC's final report to the Coastal Commission, on
July'16, 199'l , the Comission adopted a reso'lution to further condition the
permit. Three new m'itigation projects to address impacts to the marine en
vironment that the MRC found to be caused by the operations of units 2 and 3
of SoNGS were required: wetlands restoration to compensate for firh inpacts,
behavioral barriers to reduce fish impingement losses, and artificia'l reef
construction to reduce lolt resources at the San onofre Kelp Bed.
Corni ssion Responsi bi I i ti es
The permittee has the primary responsibi'lities for design'ing and imp'lementing
the three mitigation projects; however, due to the uncertainty of whether the
mitigation projects will successfully meet performance standards ident'ified'in
the permit, an independert third party monitoring, management and remediation
proqran, with Cormjssion oversjght, is also required. This "adaptive manage-
ment, program wi'll improve the likelihood of success by monitoring and, on the
basis of the data collected, regularly evaluating the progress of the mitiga-
tion projects and determining remedial steps, if necessary.
The comnission retains responsibility for approving the restoration and
construction plans, and for determining if the goals specified in the ap-
proved plans and the perfornance standards contained in the permit are being
achieved. lf the mitigation projects are not meeting these goals and stan-
dards, the comission will requjre remedial measures. Ihe comission will
convene an interagency ll,etland Advi5ory Panel and a Scientilic Advisory Panel
to assist in implementing the permit conditions. Ihe permittee is required by
the pernit to provide al'l funding necessary for the monitoring and managenent
program and the Commiss'ion's oversight responsibilitjes,
In order to carry out the monitoring and management program, the Cormission
intends to retain at least tr,ro primary consultants to provide the scientific
and technica'l expertise needed to oversee the mitigation projects and other
consultants and contractor! needed to 'implement the monitoring studies, using
funds provided by the permittee. The Cormission envisions entering into an
agreement with an appropriate foundation or nonprofit organization which would
then contract for necessary personnel and services, subject to the comis-
sion's direction and approval (see "Administrative structure," below).
The consultant scientists wou'ld tJork at the cotmission's headquarters under
the supervi5ion of the Comission's Manager and Deputy llanager of the Energy
and ocean Resources Unit. ilajor responsibilitie5 |,ould include:
(1) p'lanning (consultation on and review of site a55essments, designs,
restoratJorrand construction plans, coordination and evaluation of peer revier,,
and review by the wetlands and scientific advisory panels);




ilr. Scott Hal'labrin
January ,l4, ,]992
Page 3
(2) monitorinq studies (development of independent monitoring programs for
the wetlands restoration, reef con5truction and bar.'ier devjces projects,
oversight of monitoring contraqtor!, participation in field work, analysis and
interpretation of data, coordination of advi5ory panels and external peer
review, eva'luation of analyses and conclusions of reviewers, development of
recommendations to the Cormission) ;
(3) proqram evaluation (conducting an annual meeting for Comnission staff
to review mitigation project activities, coordination of external review,
evaluation of analyses and conc'lusions with respect to the achievement of
performance standards and remedial measures which might be required, advice to
the Executive Director on whether perfomlance standards are bejng met, recom-
nendations to the Cormis!ion for remediation)i and
(4) admin'istration (develoDment of biennial work programs and budgets,
prepirition oFnequests for Prbposals for monitoring;tu;ies, coordin;tio; of
review of contract propolals, rec o,rmendat i ons to the Executive Director on the
contractor selection, maintenance of scientific data).
Adm'i ni strati ve Structure
In order to accomplish the monitorjng, management and oversight responsibi'li-
ties under this permit, an appropriate mechanism for transferring funds from
the permittee must be e:tab'lirhed. The cormission envisions a structure in
uhich it would enter into two seDarate Memoranda of Agreement. The first
r./ould be an agreement r.rith an appropriate foundation or nonprofit organi-
zation. This fioA r,,ould establish the terms under which the foundat'ion or
nonprofit would provide services to the comnission. These services would
inc'lude (l) contractjng for consultants and other contractors, subject to the
comnission's direction and approval , needed to carry out the above tasks and
(2) establishing a special deposit account to receive funds from the permittee
to cover the costs of those tasks. Funds uould be dra!,/n from the special
account only upon the authorization of the Executive Director of the
cormi ss i on.
A separate 0A would be executed bet!,/een the Cormission and the permittee.
Thi! l40A 'rou'ld establish the work program and budget for the soi{Gs monitoring,
management and overs'ight program and rlould direct the permjttee to deposit the
necessary funds in the account established in the commission's iloA with a
foundati on or nonprofit organization.
The proposed adtninistratjve structure wou'ld meet the cornission's needs. The
conmission has the authority to contract for personnel and services necessary
to carry out its oversight, monitoring and management functions for the soNGs
nitigation projects. If the commi ssion contract! with an appropriate founda-
tion or nonprofit, the administrative function would be con5olidated into one
"master" agreement, which would maximize the efficiency of the program and
sjmplify fund transfers and other administrative procedureS, thus ninimizing
the overal'l costs to the perm'ittee. Further, the consolidation would enable
the Comis!ion to respond quickly to unforeseen c'ircumstanqes, such as the
need for additional studies or re5earch, and provide adequate flexibility to
retain appropriate consultants and contractors.


ilr. scott Hallabrin
Janua ry 14, 199?
Page 4
The Commission is currently investigating appropriate third party entities
uith uhich to enter into the master agreement. one entity !rh'ich has expressed
interest, and with which the Cormission has worked on federally-funded
projects in the Tijuana River National Estuarine Rerearch Reserve, is the San
0iego State ljniversity Foundation. The Foundat'ion is a nonprofit corporat'ion
designated as an auxiliary organization in the Education Code of the State of
California, The Foundation annually admini!ters over $50 million; it enables
University faculty to engage in sponsored research and education programs,
offers a vehicle to accept, invest and expand donated monies, and is the lega'l
"unbre'lla" under which many c ofimun i ty-ori ented activ'ities operate. Because of
the Foundation'5 expErience and mjssion, it appears that it might be an appro-
priate entity to administer the monitoring and management program for the
conmi ss i on.
conclusion
In order that the permittee riill not be considered a I'source of incomel under
Government Code section 87103.6 to the consu'ltants and contractors retajned by
the comm'ission, even though the permittee pays the fees for employing them, we
understand that the Corrnission, and not the permittee, must control the flo!,
of income to and the decisionmaking of the consultants and contractors. hle
believe that the arrangement described above would provide sufficient agency
control over the consultants' monitoring and management activities for the
mitigation projects to meet the requirements of Government Code section
87103.6 and the FPPc's regulat'ions. t,nder the "master" agreement, the com-
mjssion uould retain authority to determine which consultants and contractors
are hired, direct the irork and evaluate the performance of the consultants and
contractors, and establish the amount of compensation paid. The consultants
and contractors would be paid from a fund over wh'ich the permittee exercises
no control or authority; funds would be drawn from the account only upon the
authori zat i on of the Conmission.
The I0A between the Cortnission and the foundation or nonprofit t ould be
5eparate from the ilOA betr.,een the Cotrnis5ion and the permittee, and it t/ould
include terms which specify that compensation paid to the consultants and
contractors is dependent upon completion of the work and not dependent upon
any agency action pertaining to the mitigatio'! proiects. Simi larly, the l!l0A
between the conmission and the permittee would'inc'lude terms which specify the
amounts and schedule of payments to the account and specify that the payment
of any mitigation fees ar required by the permit conditions is not contingent
on the hiring of any specific person or consultant, is not dependent upon the
outcome of the consultants' work or their recomnendations, and is not depen-
dent upon any further agency action,
Thus, we believe that the proposed administrative structure neet5 the intended
purpose of the FPPC's regulation as well as Government Code section 87103.6,
and creates an adequate buffer between the permittee, whose mitigation fees
would pay for the employment of the consultants and contractors, and the con-
sultants and contractors, whose work would consist of revieu,ing and monitoring
the permittee's mitigati on project5,




Hr. Scott Hal Iabrin
January 14, 1992
Page 5
Please advise us at your earliest possible convenience h,hether the proposed
administrative structure for the monjtoring and management program for the
S0NGS marine resource mitigation projects is acceptable. Southern California
Edison has submitted its first report in accordance with the permit condi-
tions, and we need to proceed expeditiously to hire the scientific consultants
'in order for the mitigation projects to remain on schedule. If you have any
questions, please feel free to call Dorothy Dickey of my staff at (415)
904-5220.
Very truly yours,
